title: Privacy
text: This site is a static website hosted on GitHub Pages. It is intentionally minimal in its data handling.

title: Privacy
section: What this site does not do
text:
What this site does not do

No cookies are set by this site.
No tracking pixels, fingerprinting, or third-party advertising scripts.
No newsletter, no contact form, no account system. There is nothing to opt into.

title: Privacy
Privacy
text:
What GitHub Pages does
The site is served by GitHub Pages. GitHub may log IP addresses and other request metadata server-side as part of normal web-server operation, outside the control of this site’s editor. See GitHub’s privacy statement for details.

title: Privacy
text:
External links
External links open in the user’s browser and are subject to the privacy policy of the target site. The editor of this hub has no control over what those sites do.

title: Privacy
text:
Data subject requests
For requests under GDPR (right to access, rectification, erasure, etc.) regarding the very limited data this site itself processes (effectively none, beyond the GitHub-Pages server logs):
Contact: 58561665+r-heller@users.noreply.github.com

title: References — Molecular pathology
text: Literature underpinning CTIR’s molpathR: variant analysis, NGS file-format specifications, and clinical-genomics knowledge bases.

title: References — Molecular pathology
text:
Suggested reading

VCF format specification — Danecek et al., 2011.
BAM / SAMtools — Li et al., 2009.
GATK Best Practices — Van der Auwera et al., 2013, plus current-version updates.
ClinVar — Landrum et al., current edition.
OncoKB — Chakravarty et al., 2017.
AMP/ASCO/CAP variant-interpretation guidelines — Li et al., 2017.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References
text: A topical reading list of works underpinning the methods used across CTIR packages and the wider trauma / tissue-injury literature.

title: References
text:
Disclaimer
The lists on these pages are not an exhaustive or systematically curated bibliography. They do not necessarily reflect the full breadth of any field, and individual entries have not all been read in full or independently verified by the editor. Treat them as starting points and inspiration — pointers to further reading rather than authoritative recommendations.
For canonical references underpinning a specific CTIR package, consult that package’s inst/CITATION file or its documentation directly.
When DOI-verified citations are added to the project’s references.bib, the CI pipeline runs scripts/verify-dois.R on every push to confirm each DOI resolves.

title: References
text:
Sections

Dimensionality reduction
RNA-seq & bulk omics
Single cell & cell reports
Molecular pathology
R package development
Tidyverse
Reproducible research
Trauma biomarkers
Tissue injury

title: References — Trauma biomarkers
text: Domain-specific reading list on trauma biomarkers: discovery, validation, clinical utility.

title: References — Trauma biomarkers
text:
Suggested topics
A starting list of reading areas. Specific citations to be added over time.

Acute-phase proteins and trauma severity.
Cytokine signatures and trauma-induced coagulopathy.
Endotheliopathy of trauma — syndecan-1, thrombomodulin, and related markers.
Multi-omics biomarker discovery in polytrauma.
Reporting standards for biomarker studies — REMARK, STARD.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — RNA-seq & bulk omics
text: Foundational literature underpinning CTIR’s bambamR and adjacent bulk-omics work.

title: References — RNA-seq & bulk omics
text:
Suggested reading

DESeq2 — Love, Huber, Anders, 2014.
edgeR — Robinson, McCarthy, Smyth, 2010.
limma-voom — Law, Chen, Shi, Smyth, 2014.
STAR aligner — Dobin et al., 2013.
salmon — Patro et al., 2017.
tximport — Soneson, Love, Robinson, 2015.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — R package development
text: Reference works underpinning CTIR’s package-development tooling: hexmakR, reflowR, pressR, and the CTIR package style more generally.

title: References — R package development
text:
Reference works

Wickham, H. & Bryan, J. R Packages: Organize, Test, Document, and Share Your Code (2nd ed.). O’Reilly, 2023. https://r-pkgs.org/ (Wickham & Bryan, 2023)
R Core Team. Writing R Extensions. R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html (R Core Team, n.d.)

title: References — R package development
text:
Further reading

pkgdown — JOSS / project documentation.
roxygen2 — project documentation and design notes.
testthat — Wickham, R Journal.
Marwick et al. on research compendia (also listed under reproducible research).

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: About
text:
A curated, hand-picked hub of computational tooling and reading material for biomedical research — focused on R packaging, multi-omics analysis, reproducible workflows, and the methods underpinning trauma and tissue-injury science. Every entry has been editorially selected. Nothing on this site is auto-generated or pulled from a feed.
The hub exists to save researchers the discovery cost: instead of trawling a hundred package indexes and journal listings, you get a short, opinionated shortlist with one paragraph of context per item.

title: About
text:
A curated, hand-picked hub of computational tooling and reading material for biomedical research — focused on R packaging, multi-omics analysis, reproducible workflows, and the methods underpinning trauma and tissue-injury science. Every entry has been editorially selected. Nothing on this site is auto-generated or pulled from a feed.
The hub exists to save researchers the discovery cost: instead of trawling a hundred package indexes and journal listings, you get a short, opinionated shortlist with one paragraph of context per item.

title: About
text:
What’s excluded

Commercial vendors and paywalled tools. If the resource requires a licence to use the linked-to material, it doesn’t appear here.
Closed-source software without a credible open alternative path.
Inactive or abandoned projects — the rough cutoff is no commits or releases in the last 24 months, unless the package is feature-complete and demonstrably stable.
Marketing pages, vendor whitepapers, and aggregator blogs.

title: About
text:
Curation principles

Open source preferred. Code under an OSI-approved licence (MIT, Apache 2.0, GPL, BSD) takes precedence over closed alternatives.
Reproducible by design. Tools that make the analysis trail auditable — targets, renv, Quarto, container-based pipelines — are over-represented here on purpose.
Peer reviewed where applicable. For methods packages, a published reference is expected.
Actively maintained. Recent commits or releases, responsive issue tracker.
Verifiable provenance. Every link has been resolved at build time; every citation has been DOI-checked. Items that fail verification do not appear on the rendered site.

title: About
text:
Licence taxonomy for listed resources
Each external entry carries one of:

OSS — open-source software. The specific licence (MIT, Apache 2.0, GPL-3.0, BSD-2-Clause, etc.) is named per entry.
CC — Creative Commons content (datasets, documentation, figures). The specific variant (CC BY 4.0, CC BY-SA 4.0, CC0) is named.
PD — public domain works, including most US-government-released datasets.
OA — gold open-access publications with an explicit reuse licence.

If a resource doesn’t fit cleanly, the entry says so.

title: About
text:
Editor
Maintained by: R. Heller

title: About
text:
Inspiration
The visual language and architecture of this site are adapted from boulingua/ressources — a curated language-learning hub that solved the same editorial problem for a different domain. CTIR’s hub is monolingual (English) where boulingua is tri-lingual; the audience here is the international scientific community.

title: Acknowledgements
text:
This hub stands on the work of communities that build, maintain, and document the open scientific computing stack. With sincere thanks to:

The R Core Team and R Foundation, for sustaining the R language.
The Bioconductor project and its contributors, for the world’s most coherent biomedical analysis ecosystem.
Posit (formerly RStudio), for the IDE, the tidyverse, and Quarto.
The Quarto team, for a publishing system that takes reproducibility seriously.
The maintainers of every package referenced on this site — the work shown here is their work, surfaced and contextualised.

title: Acknowledgements
text:
This hub stands on the work of communities that build, maintain, and document the open scientific computing stack. With sincere thanks to:

The R Core Team and R Foundation, for sustaining the R language.
The Bioconductor project and its contributors, for the world’s most coherent biomedical analysis ecosystem.
Posit (formerly RStudio), for the IDE, the tidyverse, and Quarto.
The Quarto team, for a publishing system that takes reproducibility seriously.
The maintainers of every package referenced on this site — the work shown here is their work, surfaced and contextualised.

title: Acknowledgements
text:
Contributors and supporters
With sincere thanks to everyone who has contributed to, supported, or made possible the work assembled here — collaborators, students, colleagues, funders, and the open communities whose tools we depend on. The breadth of this hub is only possible because of theirs.

title: Acknowledgements
text:
Visual and architectural inspiration
The structure, navbar pattern, footer, and curated-list approach of this site are adapted from boulingua/ressources. Credit to its editor for the design language. CTIR’s hub diverges in being monolingual and in adding research-specific sections (CTIR packages, References).

title: PLAN — CTTIR/ressources
text:
Accent color: #2a6f8a (teal-blue). Distinct from boulingua’s accent; reads in light + dark.
Language: English only. Documented divergence from boulingua (which is tri-lingual because it teaches three languages; CTIR’s audience is the international scientific community).
License model: code MIT; content CC BY-SA 4.0 (mirrors boulingua).
Citation style: APA (CSL fetched at first build / committed manually).
Quarto pinned: 1.5.57 in .quarto-version.
Deploy: GitHub Pages from gh-pages branch via quarto-actions/publish@v2. Not enabled until first PR review.
Engine: freeze: true; cttir-packages.qmd uses an R chunk that reads _data/cttir-repos.json — no API calls at render time.




19 repos pulled from gh api /orgs/CTTIR/repos. Of those:

14 R packages: songR, bambamR, hexmakR, reflowR, molpathR, scimagR, segmantR, hyperspectR, cellreportR, libscanR, pressR, dynasimR, qviewparsR, plus courses and tutorials (R-flavored content repos).
5 non-package repos excluded from cttir-packages.qmd: pwa-quest, ressources (this repo), website, .github. courses and tutorials are surfaced separately in the Ressources index.
License field is NOASSERTION for most R packages — likely missing SPDX-detected LICENSE files or non-standard layout. Flagged for Raban: decide on a single license per package and ensure GitHub auto-detects it (add License: field in DESCRIPTION plus a top-level LICENSE file with the standard SPDX text).
Eight R packages have no description field on GitHub (scimagR, segmantR, hyperspectR, cellreportR, libscanR, pressR, dynasimR). The cttir-packages.qmd table renders these with a — and tags them with a build-time warning. Flagged: add GitHub repo descriptions.






These are marked TODO_IMPRESSUM or TODO_FILL_BEFORE_DEPLOY and the CI gate fails until filled:

impressum.qmd — name, postal address, email, ORCID, affiliation
privacy.qmd — contact email for data requests
about.qmd — editor name + ORCID
acknowledgements.qmd — funders, collaborators




The brief mandates DOI verification before commit. I have not populated references.bib beyond a small skeleton of book references that don’t need DOI verification (Wickham R Packages 2nd ed., R Core Team manual). All other references are deferred and the topic pages carry TODO_FILL_BEFORE_DEPLOY markers:

references/dimensionality-reduction.qmd — needs SONG paper, UMAP (McInnes 2018), t-SNE (van der Maaten 2008), PHATE (Moon 2019), PCA classics
references/rna-seq-and-bulk-omics.qmd — STAR, salmon, DESeq2 (Love 2014), edgeR (Robinson 2010), limma-voom (Law 2014)
references/single-cell-and-cell-reports.qmd — Seurat, scran, scater, SingleCellExperiment
references/molecular-pathology.qmd — VCF spec, GATK best practices, ClinVar, OncoKB
references/r-package-development.qmd — Wickham R Packages, pkgdown, roxygen2, testthat
references/reproducible-research.qmd — Marwick et al. on research compendia, targets paper, Quarto
references/trauma-biomarkers.qmd — domain-specific reading list (PubMed search needed)
references/tissue-injury.qmd — domain-specific reading list

Process for filling: for each entry, resolve DOI via https://doi.org/<doi>, confirm authors/year, add to references.bib, drop the TODO_FILL_BEFORE_DEPLOY marker on the page. The CI verify-dois job re-runs on every push and will catch any unresolvable DOI.



I did not clone each package and mine inst/CITATION / DESCRIPTION references — that requires per-repo cloning and would 10× the scope. Deferred to a follow-up pass. Recommended approach when Raban is ready:
# scripts/mine-package-citations.R
for (repo in repos) {
  gh::gh("/repos/CTTIR/{repo}/contents/inst/CITATION", repo = repo) |>
    parse_citation() |> append_to_bib()
}



Every entry in the ressources/*.qmd pages points to a canonical project URL (e.g. https://bioconductor.org, https://www.tidyverse.org). These are stable and well-known. The CI link checker will flag anything broken on first run. No [unverified] markers were needed.





Monolingual (English) — see top of file.
Accent color — #2a6f8a instead of boulingua’s accent.
Section: cttir-packages — boulingua has no analogue; this is a CTIR-specific page generated from the repo inventory.
References section — boulingua has none; CTIR’s research-hub identity makes a curated bibliography load-bearing.




Locally quarto render should succeed. CI will fail intentionally on:

TODO-marker check (Impressum / Privacy / About / Acknowledgements / References)
Possibly DOI check if any DOIs are added before verification — currently bib is minimal

This is the documented expected state until Raban fills placeholders.







H1 source: page YAML title: "#ressources" (line 2). No explicit body H1.
subtitle: "A curated hub for computational biomedical research" is also YAML-driven (line 3) — rendered inside Quarto’s title block alongside the H1.
toc: false, page-layout: full. No title-block-style override.
Body starts with prose paragraphs, then ## Sections grid, then a horizontal rule and licence line.





Site title: "#ressources" (line 12) becomes the navbar brand. The first navbar entry is Home → index.qmd (lines 26–27). Both untouched by this change.
Theme wires assets/light.scss / assets/dark.scss, both of which @import "_shared.scss" (~181 lines).





tutorials/index.qmd puts the avatar inside a ::: {.hero} block, uses a fenced {=html} raw block for the anchor, and writes the linked H1 as # [#tutorials](https://cttir.github.io/tutorials/). Title block is suppressed via title-block-style: none in YAML. The subtitle equivalent is a ::: {.lead} div (no YAML subtitle: there).
tutorials/assets/_shared.scss:205–220 holds the avatar CSS — verbatim block to mirror:
.ctir-avatar-link { display: inline-block; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }
.ctir-avatar { width: 56px; height: 56px; border-radius: 8px; object-fit: cover; display: block; }
.hero h1 a, .hero h1 a:hover { color: inherit; text-decoration: none; }





Title surface: H1 is YAML-driven, so per step 2 of the brief, remove title: from index.qmd YAML and write the H1 explicitly in the body as # [#ressources](https://cttir.github.io/ressources/).
Subtitle handling (the wrinkle): subtitle: in YAML renders inside Quarto’s auto-generated title block, which becomes empty/awkward once title: is gone. Plan: suppress the title block (title-block-style: none) and move the subtitle text into the body as ::: {.lead} — same wording, same place on the page, just rendered from body markup. Mirrors tutorials/courses for visual consistency. Reading “do not modify the subtitle” as text-preserving, not YAML-location-preserving.
Avatar markup: raw HTML inside a fenced {=html} block at the top of the body, with the exact tutorials wording (comment, aria-label, alt text, classes). Cross-site URL https://cttir.github.io/website/images/cttir-logo.png referenced live — not copied.
Wrapper: ::: {.hero} block around avatar + H1 + lead, matching tutorials. Keep existing prose paragraphs and ## Sections grid below, untouched.
CSS: append the three rules to assets/_shared.scss so both light and dark themes pick them up. Palette-free, no per-theme tweaks.





index.qmd — drop title: from YAML, add title-block-style: none, prepend .hero block with avatar + linked H1 + .lead subtitle.
assets/_shared.scss — append avatar CSS rules (mirror tutorials verbatim).
_quarto.yml — no changes.




The brief shows a simpler markup (no .hero, no .lead wrapper) in its inline example (step 3). Tutorials uses the richer wrapper. Mirroring tutorials wins on the “visual consistency across sister sites” rule, so going with the wrapper. If the bare version is preferred, easy to strip back.

title: Writing & publishing
text: Tooling for academic writing and the surrounding citation / identifier infrastructure.

title: Writing & publishing
text:
Authoring

Quarto
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Manuscripts, theses, and books from the same source as your analyses. Native cross-references, citations, and footnotes. Reach for the manuscript project type when targeting a journal.


LaTeX (TeX Live)
License: OSS · LPPL · Maintainer: TeX Users Group
The substrate for any non-trivial PDF rendering. Quarto delegates to it for PDF output; useful to know directly when the journal template diverges from defaults.


Overleaf
License: source OSS · AGPL-3.0 (community edition); hosted service is freemium · Maintainer: Overleaf / Digital Science
Collaborative LaTeX editing in the browser. The community edition is genuinely open source — listed on those grounds.


rticles
License: OSS · GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Posit
R Markdown templates for journal articles. Quarto journal templates exist separately and overlap heavily; rticles remains useful for journals that have not migrated.


bookdown · Quarto Books
License: OSS · GPL-3.0 / MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Long-form publishing. New projects should default to Quarto Books; bookdown remains for legacy works.

title: Writing & publishing
text:
References and citations

Zotero
License: OSS · AGPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Roy Rosenzweig Center / community
The reference manager. Browser-connector capture, group libraries, and a healthy plugin ecosystem.


Better BibTeX for Zotero
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Emiliano Heyns
Auto-generates stable citation keys, exports clean BibTeX/BibLaTeX, and supports auto-export to a watched .bib file. Effectively required for serious LaTeX/Quarto work with Zotero.


Citation Style Language (CSL) · CSL repository
License: CC · CC BY-SA 3.0 (styles) · Maintainer: CSL project
The XML-based citation-formatting language. The official style repository carries thousands of journal styles.

title: Writing & publishing
text:
Identifiers

ORCID
License: service · CC0 (public data) · Maintainer: ORCID, Inc.
Persistent author identifier. Required by most journals and funders; cite yours on every artefact.


ROR — Research Organization Registry
License: PD · CC0 · Maintainer: ROR community
Persistent identifier for research institutions. Use it in affiliations and dataset metadata.

title: Ressources
text: Curated lists, grouped by topic. Each entry carries a one- or two-sentence editorial note explaining why it is here.

title: Ressources
text:
Sections

CTIR packages — every public R package in the CTTIR organization, generated from the live repo inventory.
R ecosystem — tidyverse, Bioconductor, package-development tooling, and the libraries that show up across most CTIR work.
Reproducibility — Quarto, targets, renv, container images, archival.
Statistics & ML — modelling stacks for biomarkers, multi-omics, and survival analysis.
Omics & bioinformatics — domain-specific Bioconductor stacks (single cell, proteomics, metabolomics, genomics).
Writing & publishing — Quarto, LaTeX, Zotero, ORCID, ROR, journal templates.
Data sources — public datasets and APIs relevant to trauma / multi-omics work.

title: Ressources
text:
Course material from CTIR
Two CTIR repositories deliver teaching material rather than software packages and are hosted separately:

courses
Site · License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: CTTIR
Course material maintained by the CTIR group. Linked here so users land on it without searching the org by hand.


tutorials
Site · License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: CTTIR
Standalone tutorials covering practical workflows.

title: R ecosystem
text: The R libraries and projects that show up across most CTIR work. All open source, all actively maintained at the time of writing.

title: R ecosystem
text:
Core language and project

R
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 / GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: R Core Team
The language and its base distribution. Every CTIR package depends on it, directly or transitively.


Bioconductor
License: varies (Artistic-2.0 default for member packages) · Maintainer: Bioconductor Core Team
The coherent biomedical-analysis ecosystem on top of R. Twice-yearly release cycle, shared classes (SummarizedExperiment, MultiAssayExperiment, SingleCellExperiment), and a peer-review process for new packages.

title: R ecosystem
text:
Tidyverse and adjacent

tidyverse
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit / community
A meta-package that loads dplyr, tidyr, readr, purrr, tibble, stringr, forcats, and ggplot2. The de-facto data-manipulation idiom in modern R.


data.table
License: OSS · MPL-2.0 · Maintainer: data.table contributors
The high-performance alternative to dplyr. Worth reaching for when working set sizes start to hurt.

title: R ecosystem
text:
Package development

usethis · devtools
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
The package-author’s day-to-day toolkit: scaffolding, install/load workflows, release helpers.


roxygen2
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Inline documentation generator. Function-level docstrings compile to man/*.Rd. Standard across modern R packages.


testthat
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
The unit-testing framework. Edition 3 is the current default; expect snapshot tests and parallel execution.


pkgdown
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Generates documentation websites for R packages. Every CTIR package’s gh-pages site is built with it.


lintr · styler
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: lintr / styler authors
Linting and code style enforcement. Pair them in pre-commit hooks.

title: R ecosystem
text:
Pipelines and reproducibility

targets
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Will Landau / rOpenSci
Make-style pipeline framework for R. Tracks dependencies, skips unchanged steps, and parallelises across local and HPC backends. Treat it as the default for any analysis with more than ~five non-trivial stages.


renv
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Project-local package libraries with a lockfile. The minimal viable reproducibility layer for an R project.

title: R ecosystem
text:
Bioconductor flagships relevant to multi-omics

DESeq2
License: OSS · LGPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Michael Love et al.
Differential-expression analysis for RNA-seq counts using negative binomial generalised linear models with shrunken dispersion and effect size estimates.


edgeR · limma
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: WEHI Bioinformatics
The other two members of the de-facto bulk-RNA-seq differential-expression triumvirate. limma-voom remains the standard linear-model route.


SummarizedExperiment · MultiAssayExperiment
License: OSS · Artistic-2.0 · Maintainer: Bioconductor Core Team
The shared S4 containers that hold matrix-shaped assays plus row/column metadata. Most Bioconductor pipelines speak this idiom.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text: Domain-specific tooling, primarily Bioconductor. Listed by data modality.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text:
Single-cell

Seurat
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Satija lab
The dominant single-cell-RNA-seq toolkit in R. Quality control, normalisation, integration, dimensionality reduction, clustering, differential expression, and trajectory analysis in one object.


SingleCellExperiment · scran · scater
License: OSS · GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Bioconductor
The Bioconductor single-cell stack. scater for QC and visualisation; scran for normalisation, modelling, and marker detection. Pairs naturally with the OSCA online book.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text:
Bulk RNA-seq

DESeq2 · edgeR · limma
License: OSS · LGPL-3.0 / GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Love / WEHI Bioinformatics
The differential-expression triumvirate. See the R ecosystem page for context. CTIR’s bambamR wraps this stack into a streamlined pipeline.


tximport · tximeta
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Mike Love et al.
Transcript-to-gene-level summarisation from salmon / kallisto / RSEM output. tximeta adds automatic transcriptome-version metadata propagation.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text:
Proteomics

MSstats
License: OSS · Artistic-2.0 · Maintainer: MSstats developers
Statistical inference for label-free and labelled mass-spectrometry proteomics. Standard linear-mixed-model framework with explicit handling of technical replicates and batches.


limma for proteomics
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: WEHI Bioinformatics
limma’s linear-model machinery generalises cleanly to log-transformed protein-intensity matrices. Often the first tool to reach for on a proteomics dataset.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text:
Metabolomics

xcms
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Bioconductor metabolomics team
Peak detection, alignment, and grouping for LC/GC-MS metabolomics data. The reference R implementation.


MetaboAnalystR
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Xia Lab
R interface to the MetaboAnalyst pipeline. Useful for end-to-end statistical analysis when xcms output is the starting point.

title: Omics & bioinformatics
text:
Genomics infrastructure

GenomicRanges · Biostrings · GenomicFeatures
License: OSS · Artistic-2.0 · Maintainer: Bioconductor Core Team
The infrastructure layer underneath most Bioconductor pipelines: interval arithmetic, sequence containers, and annotation resources. Worth learning once; pays back across every project.


VariantAnnotation
License: OSS · Artistic-2.0 · Maintainer: Bioconductor Core Team
Read, write, and annotate VCF files. Used by CTIR’s molpathR for variant ingestion.

title: Data sources
text: Public datasets and APIs relevant to multi-omics, biomarker, and trauma / tissue-injury research. Each entry notes the licence terms because they vary widely.

title: Data sources
text:
Literature and citation

PubMed · E-utilities
License: PD (most metadata, US-government work) · Maintainer: NCBI / NLM
The biomedical-literature index and its programmatic API. Metadata is free to redistribute; full-text articles follow each publisher’s licence.


Europe PMC
License: varies; metadata under CC0 / CC BY · Maintainer: EMBL-EBI
Mirror and extension of PubMed Central with broader open-access full-text coverage and a documented REST API.

title: Data sources
text:
Genomics repositories

NCBI GEO — Gene Expression Omnibus
License: PD (US-government work) · Maintainer: NCBI
The largest functional-genomics archive: arrays, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq. Per-submission deposit terms govern reuse; most data are openly available.


ArrayExpress / BioStudies
License: CC0 (most studies) · Maintainer: EMBL-EBI
The European functional-genomics archive, now part of BioStudies. Many studies are dual-deposited with GEO.


Ensembl
License: varies; most annotation under Apache-2.0 / CC0 · Maintainer: EMBL-EBI
Comprehensive genome annotations and a stable REST API. The reference for transcript-level annotation in many R/Bioconductor pipelines.


UCSC Genome Browser
License: varies; track licences declared per dataset · Maintainer: UCSC
Genome visualisation and a large library of annotation tracks. The Table Browser exposes the underlying tables programmatically.

title: Data sources
text:
Proteins and pathways

UniProt
License: CC · CC BY 4.0 · Maintainer: UniProt Consortium (EBI / SIB / PIR)
The reference protein-sequence and functional-annotation database. Programmatic access via REST and SPARQL.


Reactome
License: CC · CC BY 4.0 · Maintainer: Reactome Consortium
Curated biological-pathway database with R access via ReactomePA.

title: Data sources
text:
Clinical

ClinicalTrials.gov
License: PD (US-government work) · Maintainer: NIH / NLM
Registry of clinical trials worldwide. The API v2 is well documented and returns structured study, eligibility, intervention, and outcome data.


OMIM — Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man
License: registration required for API; site browsable openly; redistribution restricted · Maintainer: Johns Hopkins University
Catalogue of human genes and genetic disorders. The redistribution restrictions are real — read the licence before mirroring data.


ClinVar
License: PD (NCBI submissions) · Maintainer: NCBI
Aggregated reports of relationships between human variants and phenotypes. Used directly by CTIR’s molpathR variant-annotation flow.

title: Reproducibility
text: Tools that make an analysis trail auditable from raw data to figure. The shortlist is intentionally narrow — the goal is to pick a stack and use it, not to compare twenty options.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Authoring

Quarto
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
The successor to R Markdown. Renders the same document to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, books, and websites. Native multi-language support (R, Python, Julia, Observable). This site is built with it.


R Markdown
License: OSS · GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Posit
Still actively maintained. New projects should default to Quarto; existing R Markdown projects do not need to migrate urgently.


workflowr
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: John Blischak
Builds a reproducible-by-construction website from an R-Markdown analysis directory: every page records a git commit, RNG seed, and session info. CTIR’s reflowR provides custom themes for it.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Pipelines

targets
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Will Landau / rOpenSci
See the R ecosystem page. Default choice for non-trivial R pipelines.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Environment and dependencies

renv
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
R project lockfile. Records exact package versions; restores them on another machine.


Rocker
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 (images) / project content varies · Maintainer: Rocker Project
Curated Docker images for R, including Bioconductor-flavoured images and tidyverse-preinstalled stacks. The path of least resistance to a reproducible container.

title: Reproducibility
text:
Archival and sharing

Zenodo
License: PD (service); content varies · Maintainer: CERN
DOI minting for code, data, and supplementary material. Integrates directly with GitHub for one-click archival of releases.


OSF — Open Science Framework
License: OSS · Apache-2.0 (platform); content varies · Maintainer: Center for Open Science
Project-centric hub for protocols, preregistration, code, and data. Useful for the ancillary materials that don’t fit on Zenodo.


Binder
License: OSS · BSD-3-Clause · Maintainer: Project Jupyter
Builds an executable container from a public repo and serves it as a notebook in the browser. Lowest-friction “click to run my analysis” link for reviewers.

title: Statistics & ML
text: Modelling stacks relevant to biomarker discovery, multi-omics integration, and survival analysis. Curated for the trauma / tissue-injury context — heavy on regularised models, mixed effects, and Bayesian workflows.

title: Statistics & ML
text:
Multi-omics integration

mixOmics
License: OSS · GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Kim-Anh Lê Cao et al.
Sparse multivariate methods for omics integration: PLS-DA, sPLS, DIABLO, MINT. The de-facto package when the question is “what jointly varies across two or more omics blocks?”

title: Statistics & ML
text:
Modelling frameworks

tidymodels
License: OSS · MIT · Maintainer: Posit
Modern, opinionated modelling meta-package. Resampling, tuning, recipe-based preprocessing, and a unified parsnip interface across model engines.


mlr3
License: OSS · LGPL-3.0 · Maintainer: mlr-org
Object-oriented machine-learning framework with strong support for nested resampling, pipelines, and benchmarking. The choice when method comparison or pipeline composition is the central concern.


caret
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 / GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Max Kuhn
Predecessor to tidymodels. Still widely used in legacy code and books; new projects should default to tidymodels.

title: Statistics & ML
text:
Specific model families

glmnet
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Trevor Hastie / Jerome Friedman et al.
Lasso, ridge, and elastic-net generalised linear models. The reference implementation; cited in essentially every regularised-regression methods paper.


randomForest · ranger
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 / GPL-3.0 · Maintainer: Liaw / Wright
ranger is the fast, multithreaded reimplementation; reach for it when row counts get into six figures.


xgboost
License: OSS · Apache-2.0 · Maintainer: DMLC
Gradient-boosted trees. Native R interface; the workhorse for tabular prediction baselines.

title: Statistics & ML
text:
Survival analysis

survival
License: OSS · LGPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Terry Therneau
The reference R survival library. Cox regression, parametric survival, frailty models, multi-state models — the canonical implementation.


survminer
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Alboukadel Kassambara
ggplot2 Kaplan–Meier plots, forest plots, and diagnostic plots for survival and related models. Almost mandatory for publication-ready survival figures.

title: Statistics & ML
text:
Mixed-effects and Bayesian

lme4
License: OSS · GPL-2.0 · Maintainer: Douglas Bates et al.
Linear and generalised linear mixed-effects models. The default frequentist mixed-model engine in R.


Stan · brms · rstanarm
License: OSS · BSD-3-Clause / GPL-2.0+ · Maintainer: Stan development team
Probabilistic programming for full-Bayesian inference. brms provides a lme4-flavoured formula interface that compiles to Stan; rstanarm precompiles common GLM/GLMM templates.

title: CTIR packages
text:
Every public R package in the CTTIR GitHub organization. Rendered from _data/cttir-repos.json, which is refreshed by scripts/fetch-cttir-repos.R and validated in CI.
Package
Description
License
Site




bambamR
R package for streamlined RNA-seq analysis pipelines — from count matrices to publication-ready results
—
site


cellreportR
—
—
site


dynasimR
—
—
site


hexmakR
R package for generating polished hex sticker logos for R packages using customizable templates
—
site


hyperspectR
—
—
site


libscanR
—
—
site


molpathR
Unified R platform for molecular pathology data — parse NGS files (VCF, BAM, FASTQ), XML/PDF reports, and clinical data into a queryable database with an interactive Shiny app for clinical exploration and visualization.
—
site


pressR
—
—
site


qviewparsR
Pure-R parser for the binary .Q-View project file format used in chemiluminescent multiplex ELISA plate imaging and quantification.
—
site


reflowR
Custom workflowr themes and templates for reproducible research websites in R
—
site


scimagR
—
—
site


segmantR
—
—
site


songR
R package for SONG dimensionality reduction — structured, interpretable embeddings for high-dimensional biological data
—
site

title: CTIR packages
text:
Notes

License — means GitHub’s licence detector did not find an SPDX-identified LICENSE file in the repository’s expected location. This is editorial debt to resolve in each package.
Description — means the GitHub repository description field is blank.
The list is generated at build time. To refresh the underlying inventory, run Rscript scripts/fetch-cttir-repos.R and commit the updated _data/cttir-repos.json.

title: #ressources
text:
A curated hub for computational biomedical research
This site is a hand-picked, editorially reviewed collection of tools, packages, datasets, and reading material relevant to CTIR — Computational Trauma and Tissue Injury Research. It is not an aggregator and not auto-generated. Every entry was selected because it earned its place.
The focus is on open, reproducible, peer-reviewed work in R packaging, multi-omics, statistical modelling, and the broader ecosystem that supports trauma and tissue-injury research.

title: #ressources
text:
Sections


About
What this hub is, what it excludes, and the editorial principles behind every entry.


Ressources
Curated lists of CTIR packages, the surrounding R ecosystem, reproducibility tooling, statistical methods, omics infrastructure, writing tools, and public data sources.


References
A topical bibliography underpinning the methods used across CTIR packages and the wider trauma / tissue-injury literature.


Acknowledgements
Credits to the maintainers, communities, funders, and collaborators that make this work possible.



Code on this site is released under the MIT licence. Editorial content is released under CC BY-SA 4.0.

title: References — Tissue injury
text: Domain-specific reading list on tissue injury: mechanisms, repair, pathology, computational modelling.

title: References — Tissue injury
text:
Suggested topics
A starting list of reading areas. Specific citations to be added over time.

Damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) and sterile inflammation.
Tissue regeneration versus fibrotic repair.
Histopathology scoring systems and inter-rater variability.
Spatial transcriptomics in injury contexts.
Computational models of wound healing and repair dynamics.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — Dimensionality reduction
text: Foundational and current literature on dimensionality reduction methods relevant to CTIR’s songR and adjacent packages: SONG, UMAP, t-SNE, PHATE, PCA.

title: References — Dimensionality reduction
text:
Suggested reading

SONG — Tang et al. The primary method paper for the technique implemented in songR.
UMAP — McInnes, Healy, Melville.
t-SNE — van der Maaten and Hinton.
PHATE — Moon et al.
Foundational PCA references in the multivariate-analysis context.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — Single cell & cell reports
text: Literature underpinning CTIR’s cellreportR and adjacent single-cell methods.

title: References — Single cell & cell reports
text:
Suggested reading

Seurat — Hao et al., current edition.
SingleCellExperiment and the Bioconductor single-cell stack — Amezquita et al., 2020.
scran normalisation — Lun, McCarthy, Marioni, 2016.
scater QC — McCarthy, Campbell, Lun, Wills, 2017.
Orchestrating Single-Cell Analysis with Bioconductor (OSCA) — online book.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — Reproducible research
text:
Marwick, B., Boettiger, C., Mullen, L. Packaging Data Analytical Work Reproducibly Using R (and Friends). The American Statistician, 2018.
Landau, W. M. The targets R package: a dynamic Make-like function-oriented pipeline toolkit. JOSS, 2021.
Sandve et al. Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research. PLOS Computational Biology, 2013.
Wilson et al. Best Practices for Scientific Computing. PLOS Biology, 2014.
Quarto — project documentation and methods notes.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — Reproducible research
text:
Marwick, B., Boettiger, C., Mullen, L. Packaging Data Analytical Work Reproducibly Using R (and Friends). The American Statistician, 2018.
Landau, W. M. The targets R package: a dynamic Make-like function-oriented pipeline toolkit. JOSS, 2021.
Sandve et al. Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research. PLOS Computational Biology, 2013.
Wilson et al. Best Practices for Scientific Computing. PLOS Biology, 2014.
Quarto — project documentation and methods notes.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.

title: References — Tidyverse
text: The full tidyverse framework — the meta-package, its core members, and the surrounding r-lib toolchain that most modern R analyses lean on.

title: References — Tidyverse
text:
Meta and design

Wickham et al. Welcome to the tidyverse. JOSS, 2019. The framing paper for the family.
Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, Grolemund. R for Data Science (2nd ed.). O’Reilly, 2023. The canonical entry-point text.

title: References — Tidyverse
text:
Core packages

dplyr — grammar of data manipulation.
tidyr — tidy-data shape transformations.
readr — fast, friendly reading of rectangular data.
tibble — modern reimagining of the data frame.
purrr — functional programming with consistent type contracts.
stringr — string manipulation, ICU-backed.
forcats — tools for working with factors.
ggplot2 — grammar of graphics.
lubridate — date and date-time arithmetic.

title: References — Tidyverse
text:
Foundational papers

Wickham. Tidy Data. Journal of Statistical Software, 2014. The conceptual basis for tidyr and the tidyverse data shape.
Wickham. A Layered Grammar of Graphics. Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics, 2010. The basis for ggplot2.

title: References — Tidyverse
text:
Adjacent r-lib toolchain
These are not part of the tidyverse meta-package but share its API conventions and maintainers:

rlang — tidy-eval foundations.
vctrs — typed vector primitives shared across the tidyverse.
glue — string interpolation.
fs — cross-platform filesystem operations.
httr2 — modern HTTP client.

See the disclaimer — this is a starting list, not a curated bibliography.
